name: Big Five ↔ Media Preference (Rentfrow/Goldberg/Zilca and the trait×content literature)
core_claim: Stable individual differences in personality — captured by the five-factor model (Openness/Intellect, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Neuroticism/Emotional Stability) — systematically predict what content people prefer. The central claim for a recommender is that PREFERENCE IS NOT A PROPERTY OF THE CONTENT ALONE; it is the output of a person×content interaction. The same film can be enjoyed or disliked depending on the trait profile of the viewer. The most robust, replicated links are: Openness to Experience → preference for complex, abstract, unconventional, ambiguous, intellectually/aesthetically demanding material (arthouse, foreign film, documentary, classical/jazz, philosophy, surreal/non-linear narrative) and against the plain, obvious and conventional; Openness/Intellect also → 'Dark' and 'Cerebral' content. Extraversion (specifically its excitement-seeking facet, overlapping sensation-seeking) → arousing, social, upbeat, high-stimulation content ('Communal' and, more weakly/inconsistently, 'Thrilling'). Agreeableness → conventional, warm, relationship-centred, prosocial content (romance, family, pop, 'Communal'); negatively related to 'Dark'. Conscientiousness → informational/wholesome content and against 'Dark'/edgy/hedonistic material. Neuroticism/Emotional Stability has weak and inconsistent direct genre links but matters for mood-driven selection (anxious viewers may avoid or seek high-arousal content depending on regulation strategy). The keystone empirical structure is Rentfrow, Goldberg & Zilca (2011), who showed cross-medium entertainment preferences collapse into five dimensions — Communal, Aesthetic, Dark, Thrilling, Cerebral — each with a distinct personality signature.
descriptive_vs_experiential: This framework operates at a THIRD level distinct from both descriptive (what a story is about) and experiential (what it feels like): it is a PERSON-side / individual-differences framework. The Big Five describe the VIEWER, not the work. Its contribution to a media taxonomy is relational — it specifies which stable viewer traits pull toward which content properties. It therefore sits orthogonally to a topical theme taxonomy and to an affect taxonomy: it is the mapping function, not a set of content tags. That said, the five Rentfrow entertainment-preference dimensions (Communal/Aesthetic/Dark/Thrilling/Cerebral) ARE content-side groupings and are partly descriptive-plus-experiential blends (e.g. 'Aesthetic' fuses topic — art/foreign film — with a felt quality of demandingness; 'Dark' fuses content — horror/heavy-metal — with an experiential intensity/edginess). So the framework spans two layers: a person layer (the Big Five themselves) and a derived content-grouping layer (the five preference dimensions) that the traits predict.
key_dimensions: PERSON-SIDE AXES (Big Five / OCEAN): (1) Openness to Experience / Intellect — imagination, aesthetic sensitivity, intellectual curiosity, tolerance of ambiguity, preference for complexity and novelty. (2) Conscientiousness — orderliness, dutifulness, self-discipline. (3) Extraversion — sociability, positive affect, and the excitement-seeking facet (high-arousal stimulation seeking). (4) Agreeableness — warmth, trust, conventionality, prosociality. (5) Neuroticism (vs. Emotional Stability) — anxiety, negative affect, threat sensitivity. CONTENT-SIDE AXES (Rentfrow et al. 2011, five entertainment-preference dimensions): (a) Communal — relationships/people: romance, family film, daytime talk, reality TV, pop. (b) Aesthetic — creative, abstract, cultured, dense, demanding: classical/jazz, foreign film, poetry, art. (c) Dark — intensity, edginess, hedonism: horror, heavy-metal/punk, erotica. (d) Thrilling — action, adventure, suspense, fantasy: action/adventure, spy, sci-fi. (e) Cerebral — information-oriented: news, business, health, educational. Adjacent constructs that sharpen the person side: sensation-seeking / need-for-arousal (overlaps Extraversion excitement-seeking + Openness experience-seeking), and need-for-cognition (overlaps Openness; predicts tolerance for complex, ambiguous narrative).
cross_cultural_validity: The Big Five themselves replicate broadly across many languages and cultures (large NEO-PI-R and lexical cross-cultural programmes), but Openness/Intellect is repeatedly flagged as the LEAST cross-culturally stable factor — its definition, lexical realisation, and even its emergence as a single coherent factor vary in non-Western, less-educated, and less-literate samples, where it sometimes splinters or merges with other dimensions. [uncertain] The media-preference SIDE of the framework is far more Western/English-bound than the trait model: Rentfrow, Goldberg & Zilca (2011) explicitly note their samples (University of Texas, Eugene-Springfield community, internet) were 'predominantly Caucasian and middle class' and call generalisability a limitation. The specific genre groupings (the five entertainment dimensions) reflect a US/Western media ecology — the cultural meaning of 'heavy metal' as 'Dark' or 'reality TV' as 'Communal' is not guaranteed to transfer. So: the trait inputs are reasonably universal except Openness; the trait→genre mappings are demonstrated mainly in Western, anglophone, middle-class samples and should be treated as culturally provisional. Cross-cultural film-genre studies exist but are smaller and less consistent.
empirical_grounding: Among the strongest empirically-grounded frameworks in this set, but the evidence is overwhelmingly CORRELATIONAL/survey, not behavioural or experimental at scale. Rentfrow, Goldberg & Zilca (2011, Journal of Personality) is the keystone: N ≈ 3,227 across three samples (1,946 undergraduates, 736 community residents, 545 internet), factor-analytically derived the five preference dimensions, replicated the structure across samples/methods/regions, and showed personality explained significant variance over and above demographics. Reported effect sizes are moderate for the headline links (Openness→Aesthetic r ≈ .38–.44; Openness→Cerebral r ≈ .15–.19; Openness negative to Communal; Agreeableness positive to Communal; low Conscientiousness/low Agreeableness + high Openness for Dark) and WEAK/INCONSISTENT for Extraversion→Thrilling (r ranged −.09 to .10 across samples — a key caution against over-trusting 'Extraversion→arousing'). The broader genre-personality literature (e.g. the 2024–25 film-genre/personality cross-sectional studies) replicates direction but with small, sometimes noisy effects and occasional reversals (horror linked to extraversion AND agreeableness in some samples). Sensation-seeking↔Extraversion-excitement-seeking and Openness↔need-for-cognition links are well established. WHAT IS MISSING: almost no large-scale validation against actual completion/watch-through/joint-choice behaviour; effects are population-level tendencies with large within-trait variance, so individual prediction is weak. To validate LLM-extracted CONTENT dimensions against this framework you would need: (a) gold content labels for the five preference dimensions (or for trait-relevant content properties like complexity/arousal/conventionality), (b) viewer Big Five scores, (c) real preference/completion data, then test whether trait×content interaction terms predict choice better than content main effects alone.
operationalisability: SPLIT, and the split is the whole point. The PERSON side (Big Five scores) is NOT extractable from a synopsis at all — it requires viewer-side data (a personality inventory, or inference from ratings history / onboarding). The CONTENT side is partially extractable. LLM-from-synopsis can estimate trait-relevant CONTENT PROPERTIES with moderate reliability: narrative complexity / ambiguity / non-linearity / intellectual density (the Openness signal), conventionality vs. unconventionality, prosocial/relationship-centred warmth (Agreeableness/Communal signal), edginess/transgression/hedonism/horror intensity (Dark signal), and action/adventure/high-stimulation density (the arousal/Thrilling signal). Extraction unit: whole work is natural for genre-level signals; arc/episode level for serialised content where intensity varies. FAILURE MODES: (1) The headline 'Openness→complexity' is reliable in DIRECTION but the synopsis often cannot tell genuine narrative complexity/ambiguity from a marketing claim of 'mind-bending'; (2) Arousal/stimulation is partly an execution/pacing property invisible to plot summary; (3) Sensitive content (erotica, gore) is frequently sanitised in synopses, deflating the 'Dark' read; (4) Conventionality is hard to judge without genre context; (5) MOST IMPORTANTLY, even perfect content extraction yields only half the equation — the predictive value comes from CROSSING content properties with viewer traits, and the viewer trait half cannot come from synopsis. Treat all content extractions as priors over content properties, never as personality.
motivation_served: Spans several motivations because traits route to different needs: Openness maps to eudaimonic/cognitive-aesthetic appreciation (meaning, complexity, novelty, being intellectually and aesthetically stimulated); Extraversion/sensation-seeking maps to hedonic high-arousal enjoyment and social connection (fun, excitement, shared upbeat experience); Agreeableness maps to warmth, prosocial connection and social-bonding viewing; Neuroticism maps to mood regulation (selection for calming or for cathartic high-arousal content depending on the person's regulation style). For GROUP viewing this is among the most directly useful frameworks in the set: a two-person pick is literally a trait×trait×content optimisation. The classic mismatch is a high-Openness viewer (wants demanding/ambiguous) paired with a low-Openness/high-Agreeableness viewer (wants warm/conventional/clear) — the framework predicts the conflict AND the compromise zone (a well-crafted, warm-but-substantial crowd-pleaser that scores moderate on complexity and high on relational warmth). Extraversion mismatches (one wants a loud thrill-ride, one wants something quiet) map cleanly to a content-arousal axis the recommender can trade off.
receiver_dependence: Maximally receiver-dependent — this is the framework's defining feature and its main lesson for a two-person recommender. The Big Five axes are pure VIEWER properties; they say nothing about a work in isolation. The PREFERENCE is the emergent product of viewer-trait × content-property, not a property of either alone. Contrast with absolute content axes (gore level, runtime) which are work-only: an Openness→complexity match is RELATIONAL — 'complex' is a content property, but whether complexity is a positive or negative is set by the viewer's Openness. So encode THREE separable things: (a) work-level content properties (complexity, conventionality, arousal/stimulation, relational-warmth, edginess) — extractable, roughly absolute; (b) per-viewer Big Five / preference-dimension profile — from onboarding or ratings inference, NOT from any synopsis; (c) the interaction — a match score that crosses (a) against (b). For pairs, the right object is a joint function over both viewers' trait profiles and the work's content-property vector. The single most actionable takeaway: do not bake 'good/bad' into content tags — store neutral content properties and let viewer traits supply the sign.
blind_spots: (1) Effects are population-level and moderate-to-weak; within-trait variance is large, so individual prediction from traits alone is poor — traits are a prior, not a verdict. (2) Extraversion→Thrilling, the intuitive 'extraverts like arousing content', is actually one of the WEAKEST and least consistent links in Rentfrow et al. (r near zero in places) — a caution against the very stereotype this framework is invoked for. (3) Traits are stable but viewing is also state/mood-driven (the same person picks differently when stressed vs. relaxed) — the Big Five miss within-person temporal variation that mood-management theory captures. (4) It is content-agnostic on the viewer side and only coarsely content-aware on the work side — it cannot tell you WHY a specific story works, only which broad content cluster a trait pulls toward. (5) Strong Western/middle-class sampling and Openness's cross-cultural instability limit transfer. (6) It under-models social-context effects (who you watch WITH changes choice), fandom/parasocial attachment, nostalgia, and habit/familiarity. (7) Genre-level groupings are coarse — 'Dark' lumps horror with erotica with heavy-metal; useful as a prior, lossy as a recommendation unit. (8) It does not address completion/rewatch — only stated preference.
vocabulary_implications: Do NOT add Big Five traits to the theme vocabulary — they are VIEWER attributes and belong in a per-user profile, not the content taxonomy. Concrete proposals: (A) Add a small set of neutral, cross-cutting CONTENT-PROPERTY facets (work-level, LLM-extractable as priors, NOT in the topical theme list): narrative-complexity (low–high: plain/linear vs. ambiguous/non-linear/dense — the Openness lever), stimulation-intensity (calm vs. high-arousal action/spectacle — the Extraversion/sensation lever), conventionality (conventional/familiar vs. unconventional/experimental — Openness lever), relational-warmth (cold/cerebral vs. warm/relationship-centred — Agreeableness/Communal lever), edginess-transgression (wholesome vs. edgy/hedonistic/explicit — the Dark lever, low Conscientiousness/low Agreeableness). (B) Add a per-USER profile (separate data source, from onboarding inventory or ratings inference): big_five_profile or, more pragmatically, the five Rentfrow preference-dimension scores (communal, aesthetic, dark, thrilling, cerebral). FLAG CLEARLY: the user-side fields are NOT extractable from any synopsis — they need a personality short-form (e.g. TIPI/BFI-10) at onboarding or inference from rating behaviour. (C) Compute match as content-property × user-trait interactions, and for pairs, a joint score across both users' profiles. (D) Mark stimulation-intensity and narrative-complexity as PARTIALLY execution-dependent (pacing/editing not fully in synopsis) so they are treated as soft priors. Critically: keep content facets sign-neutral; the user trait supplies whether a property is a plus or minus.
relationship_to_existing_vocabulary: The framework is largely ORTHOGONAL to the existing slug list (which is topical/structural) and reframes how it should be USED rather than adding many topic slugs. It VALIDATES that the 'Texture' cluster gestures at the right content properties an Openness-driven viewer responds to: nonlinear-storytelling, slow-burn, magical-realism, absurdist-comedy, character-study, meditation-on-time, meditation-on-death map onto the high-complexity / high-Openness / 'Aesthetic'+'Cerebral' end; these are the slugs a high-Openness viewer is pulled toward and a low-Openness viewer pushed away from. The Speculative cluster (existential-horror, cosmic-horror, folk-horror, technology-runs-amok) and War/violence map onto the 'Dark' and 'Thrilling' dimensions. Relationship/emotion slugs (found-family, friendship-tested, forbidden-love, coming-of-age, platonic-soulmates) map onto 'Communal'/relational-warmth (Agreeableness pull). It reveals GAPS that are NOT topic slugs but cross-cutting content-property facets the current vocabulary lacks: there is no narrative-complexity axis, no stimulation-intensity/arousal axis, no conventionality axis, and no relational-warmth axis — yet these are exactly the levers the Big Five act on. The bigger structural implication: the existing slugs encode WHAT a story is; this framework says preference also needs WHO is watching, so the system must pair a content-property layer with a per-user trait layer and score the interaction. It does not suggest removing slugs; it suggests adding a neutral content-property facet set plus a user-trait profile, and never treating any slug as universally 'liked'.
key_sources: Rentfrow, P. J., Goldberg, L. R., & Zilca, R. (2011). 'Listening, Watching, and Reading: The Structure and Correlates of Entertainment Preferences.' Journal of Personality, 79(2), 223–258. KEYSTONE; free full text on PMC: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2964424/ (also Wiley, paywalled: https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/j.1467-6494.2010.00662.x). Rentfrow, P. J., Goldberg, L. R., & Levitin, D. J. (2011). 'The Structure of Musical Preferences: A Five-Factor Model (MUSIC).' Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 100(6), 1139–1157 (ResearchGate copy: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/49815537). 'Association Between One's Preferred Film Genres and Personality Traits: A Cross-Sectional Study' (recent, open access PMC: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC12182957/). Aluja, García & García (2003) 'Relationships among extraversion, openness to experience, and sensation seeking,' Personality and Individual Differences (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0191886902002441, paywalled). Hall, A. 'Big Five Personality Traits and Media Use,' International Encyclopedia of Media Psychology (Wiley, paywalled: https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1002/9781119011071.iemp0246). On Openness as least cross-culturally stable + Openness/need-for-cognition/ambiguity-tolerance: Openness to Experience (Wikipedia overview with citations: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Openness_to_experience); 'To fear or fly to the unknown: Tolerance for ambiguity and Big Five personality traits,' Journal of Research in Personality (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0092656619300236, paywalled). STOMP/STOMP-R scales (Gosling lab, freely available: https://gosling.psy.utexas.edu/scales-weve-developed/short-test-of-music-preferences-stomp/).
